issue_title: No default encoding (2.0.0a1)
issue_body:
I kept getting the error `AttributeError: 'DatabaseWrapper' object has no attribute 'encoding'` and it turns out that encoding is only set by default [in this block of base.py](https://github.com/lionheart/django-pyodbc/blob/django-2/django_pyodbc/base.py#L184) if there is an OPTIONS key who's value is a dictionary with least one key.

Workaround: add an OPTIONS key to your config that has a populated dictionary

Possible solutions:
1. Check and set [base.py](https://github.com/lionheart/django-pyodbc/blob/django-2/django_pyodbc/base.py#L202) after line 202 with something like
```if not self.encoding:
        self.encoding = 'utf-8'
```
2. Change [line 394 in base.py](https://github.com/lionheart/django-pyodbc/blob/django-2/django_pyodbc/base.py#L394) from 'return CursorWrapper(cursor, self.driver_supports_utf8, self.encoding, self)' to 'return CursorWrapper(cursor, self.driver_supports_utf8, getattr(self, 'encoding'), self)

CursorWrapper's __init__ function is ready to handle None/empty string values, so I'm not sure which solution works better behind the goals of the project.  I figure imposing a default encoding without

issue_title: Regression: Failure on options in layer.yaml
issue_body:
## Checklist

 - [X] Confirmed this is an issue with charm-tools, not charmstore-client
 - [X] Provide versions of tools used
 - [X] Described the feature or ways to replicate the issue

## What version am I running?

I ran the following command: `charm version` and got the following output:

_It's complicated._

Using the snap:
```
$ which charm
/snap/bin/charm
$ charm version
charm error
charm-tools 2.1.2
```
Using the deb from universe:
```
$ which charm
/usr/bin/charm
$ charm version
charm 2.1.1-0ubuntu1
charm-tools 2.1.2
```

The issue is also present in `master` at `36d72f31dc50ca7fb707697a1608f6c94d0c142e`.
I am using: xenial

## Issue/Feature

Trying to build a charm I get:
```
$ charm build
build: Composing into /home/bac/charms
build: Destination charm directory: /home/bac/charms/builds/bundleservice
build: Processing layer: layer:basic
build: Processing layer: layer:apt
build: Processing layer: layer:nagios
build: Processing layer: bundleservice
build: Processing interface: nrpe-external-master
charmtools.build.tactics: Options set for undefined layer: apt
```
and nothing is created in the `build` directory.


### I expect/expected the following

The charm command should not have complained and the charm should be built.

### What I got

See above.

The error is produced at: 

https://github.com/juju/charm-tools/blob/master/charmtools/build/tactics.py#L516

Debugging I see that `defined_layer_names` includes `layer:basic` and `layer:apt`.  The `option_layer_names` includes `apt` with no 'layer' prefix. This is the crux of the problem.

The layer.yaml file is:

```
includes: ['layer:basic', 'layer:apt', 'layer:nagios', 'interface:nrpe-external-master']
repo: https://github.com/juju/bundleservice-charm.git
options:
  apt:
    packages:
      - bundle service
```

This naming follows the example in 
https://jujucharms.com/docs/2.0/reference-layer-yaml#layer-options

If, however, we temporarily change the name in `options` to `layer:apt` then the charm builds ok.

Also note that if I install version 2.1.4 of charm-tools from pip then I can use `charm-build` and it works as expected.

issue_title: No timeout for docker execute
issue_body:
Hi,

We are wotking on a script to automate some stuff using docker and docker-py in some point we execute some code inside docker, this command can take from a few minutes to a 5 or 6 hours.

I realized that I could do something like this:

```python
container_name = 'container_name_or_id'
cli = Client(timeout=None)
output = cli.execute(container_name, 'ls')
```

And works as expected, but then the container have to be stopped and started (here I just put the specific code for a PoC):

```python
from docker import Client

container_name = 'container_name_or_id'
cli = Client(timeout=None)

# Here goes a command that take a lot of time, not just a ls
output = cli.execute(container_name, 'ls')
cli.stop(container_name)
# Do some more stuff
cli.start(container_name)
```

But we get this:

```
Traceback (most recent call last):
  File "test_resume.py", line 48, in <module>
    cli.stop(container_name)
  File "/home/truiz/deployer/venv/lib/python2.7/site-packages/docker/client.py", line 923, in stop
    timeout=(timeout + self.timeout))
TypeError: unsupported operand type(s) for +: 'int' and 'NoneType'
```

Is there any other way of setting an "infinite" timeout? or just no timeout for this long runnig scripts?

Regards

```
15:48:03: Start scanning...

The GAP ADV timeout of 10 seconds is correct. I use the following code on an nRF51 dongle which is flashed automatically using the library (pc_ble_driver_sd_api_v2):

I am not sure how to debug this. Could you help me? The value of the `BLEGapTimeoutSrc` changes for every run.

issue_title: database: Better understand the window of loss (and loss scenarios) for database backups
issue_body:
The database component uses [wal-e](https://github.com/wal-e/wal-e) to ship backups and WAL logs to the deis-store component. Full backups occur by default every 3 hours, and WAL logs are shipped continuously. When a database comes up fresh on a host, it first asks WAL-e if there are any backups to restore. If so, it restores the backup and then begins to replay the WAL logs that were written since the backup.

There is potentially a small window of loss here if the database component should exit before the last WAL log is written to disk. Additionally, users (including @lorieri) have occasionally reported loss of some data when upgrading Deis or otherwise performing a database restore. We test Deis platform upgrades for every Deis release, and this includes a database restore. However, the amount of data we're storing is not large. There is likely either a larger loss window than we think, or other causes of data loss. 

Ceph doesn't seem to be the blame, as registry data (which is also stored in Ceph) is reported to be fully intact. This points to a combination of the database backup loop logic and wal-e itself. We should so some investigation to better attempt to replicate what users are sometimes seeing in real-world environments.

Somewhat related is the fragility of the backup loop in the bin/boot script: #2741

issue_title: Design Doc: Metrics and Telemetry system
issue_body:
## Goals
 * Allow out of box installations of Deis to easily monitor and alert on the state of the cluster.
 * Handle 3rd party integrations for all aspects of the metrics and telemetry system (storage of time series data, graphing, and alerting).

## Code Changes
 * Instrumentation of all Deis components will be necessary.

## Tests
 * Will determine this once the scope of changes is determined. Ideally we should start a cluster and run load tests against it and expect to see alerts generated based on that load.

## Current Design
### Collecting metrics and events
Before any metric can be graphed or alert sent data needs to be collected and processed. Passing all of this data through 1 system allows the user to craft rules not only around metrics but also events occuring within the cluster itself. For example:

```
if a deploy has just occurred and load is above 2
  do not alert
end
```

issue_title: personal_importRawKey should NOT strip leading 0x from private key.
issue_body:
* Version: 4.2.1
* Python: 3.6.5
* OS: linux


### What was wrong?
When importing a custom account, the RPC JSON strips the leading `0x` characters from the private key. This causes [ganache](https://github.com/trufflesuite/ganache-cli) to return an error because it expects the leading `0x` when parsing the data. This may, of course, be an error on ganache's part rather than web3's in which case please let me know and I will re-issue it accordingly! The RPC is as follows:

```js
personal_importRawKey
   > {
   >   "jsonrpc": "2.0",
   >   "method": "personal_importRawKey",
   >   "params": [
   >     "59afed4855441d80384bb2bebcb29e03b4892ca21e93505ab7345588b245f1c7",
   >     "test passphrase"
   >   ],
   >   "id": 5
   > }
 <   {
 <     "id": 5,
 <     "jsonrpc": "2.0",
 <     "error": {
 <       "message": "private key length is invalid",
 <       "code": -32000,
 <       "data": {
 <         "stack": "RangeError: private key length is invalid\n..."
```

### How can it be fixed?
In `middleware/pythonic.py`, the following formatters are applied to this API call:

```python
        # personal
        'personal_importRawKey': apply_formatter_at_index(
            compose(remove_0x_prefix, hexstr_if_str(to_hex)),
            0,
        ),
```

It should be removed entirely.

issue_title: How to specialize a package's runtime requirements during a build?
issue_body:
When a package has a dependency, but is very flexible about which version of that dependency is used, one would define the recipe's build and run requirements very generally. For example, the `hdf5` recipe needs `zlib`, but the version doesn't matter much. Therefore, https://github.com/conda/conda-recipes/blob/master/hdf5/meta.yaml simply specifies:

```
package:
  name: hdf5
  version: 1.8.14

requirements:
  build:
    - zlib
  run:
    - zlib
```

However, once hdf5 is built against a _specific_ version of zlib (say, `zlib-1.2.8`), the run requirement must be restricted to zlib versions that are binary compatible with the one used during the build. This is indeed the case in official anaconda packages (the ones I get by calling `conda install hdf5`), whose `index.json` specifies

```
"depends": [
    "zlib 1.2*"
  ],
  "requires": [
    "zlib 1.2.8"
  ], 
```

However, I have not found any documented way to achieve an automatic specialization from `zlib` to `zlib 1.2*` in one's own recipes and builds. What am I missing?

`conda activate root
conda info
set CONDA_SUBDIR=win-32
conda create -n newenv_win32
conda activate new_env_win32
conda info
conda activate root
conda info`

The platform returned by the `conda info` calls was win-64, then win-32, then win-32. 

Is this intended ? Am I supposed to change CONDA_SUBDIR manually, or are there better ways to create 32 bits environments ?

issue_title: Add conda signal handler
issue_body:
In conda 4.2 we added a generalized exception handler (`exceptions.py:conda_exception_handler`).  We should also add "middleware" for a signal handler.

For example, we should consciously think through how we handle `ctrl-c` / `SIGINT`.  At a minimum we should attempt to clear all lock files before exiting.

issue_title: Updating r and rstudio. RStudio does not start
issue_body:
I have been having Anaconda for about a year. I recently tried to update r from version 3.4.1 to 3.4.2 and RStudio upto version 1.1.383. On both systems OS X el capitan and Centos 7 the update was successful but RStudio failed to run! ON OS X it did show empty warning window whereas on Centos 7 it was asking for an object file!
I could not find anything in internet on this issue and decided to reinstall anaconda completely, now having version 5.0.1. I created a new environment and run `conda install r --channel r` and `conda install rstudio --channel r`. This is when RStudio begun to work again.

issue_title: Synchronous motor movements
issue_body:
Hello,

I was wondering how to control the Ax12A servos synchronously, not asynchronously. I've been using  dxl_io.set_goal_position, but the program does not execute this synchronously. The program calls this line of code and then immediately moves to the next line, without waiting for the servo to get to that position. I was wondering if there is a function that tells the servo to go to a position and does not go to the next line of code until the servo is there. 

Isn't that a better way to control a robot anyway? With the set_goal_position function, how can I tell servo 1 to go to 30 degrees and then only after that make servo 2 go to 120 degrees. Would you have to use a time.sleep() function? There's got to be a better way.

Thank you so much

issue_title: Curator API Not Returning ".monitoring" Indices
issue_body:
Elasticsearch: 5.2.1
Curator: 4.2.6

Been using curator to prune our Log cluster, however we recently started storing Monitoring data from xPack there. I wanted to start pruning those indices as well.

We use a slightly modified version of [this code](https://gist.github.com/jarpy/f65b24209e0a9240207599a73ce99bc9#file-serverless-curator-py) from [this blog post](https://www.elastic.co/blog/serverless-elasticsearch-curator-on-aws-lambda).

Currently all the logs are getting cleaned up, however when adding the prefix `.monitoring-es-2-` to the cleanup list, nothing occurs.

I checked the indices being pulled in by the initial IndexList creation, and its all of our logs indices, but none of the indices that start with `.`.

Code snippet in question:
```
index_list = curator.IndexList(es)
print (index_list.working_list())
```

That just returns all indices except those with starting with `.`. Is there some way to override that, that I may have missed in the docs somewhere? Or is this something by design? Or a bug? or PEBKAC?

issue_title: Remove 'pyopenssl' requirement from setup.py
issue_body:
It's a package that has dependencies that are 'difficult' to install, and only useful if you want to use `get_certificate_from_publish_settings`.

There's an alternate way of creating a .pem file using CLI as described here:
https://github.com/Azure/azure-content/issues/1738

We shouldn't force everyone to install it.  If it's not clear by looking at the import error from:
```
import OpenSSL.crypto as crypto
```
We could try/except and have a clearer message such as "pyopenssl package is required"

I recently got a report from a user that couldn't install azure package on raspberry pi due to pyopenssl.

issue_title: set_container_acl erase the current container access policies.
issue_body:
### Which service(blob, file, queue) does this issue concern?
blob.
I'm trying to generate 2 tokens, Read-only and Read-Write, for the container.

### What problem was encountered?
when using set_container_acl() to set a stored access policy to container, it always clear all the existing container access policies first, then set the new policy.

here is my code:
----------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------
def generate_SAS():

        service = BlockBlobService(account_name=account_name, account_key=account_key)
        
        service.create_container(container_name)
        
        # Set access policy on container
        
        access_policy = AccessPolicy(permission=container_permissions, expiry=datetime.utcnow() + timedelta(days=+days_until_expiry))
        
        identifiers = {id_number: access_policy}
        
        acl = service.set_container_acl(container_name, signed_identifiers=identifiers)
       
        # Wait 30 seconds for acl to propagate
        time.sleep(30)
       
        # Indicates to use the access policy set on the container
        token = service.generate_container_shared_access_signature(container_name,id=id_number)  
       
        print('READ TOKEN IS :')
        print(token)
       
if __name__ == '__main__':
    account_name   =os.environ['STORAGE_ACCOUNT_NAME']
    account_key    =os.environ['STORAGE_ACCOUNT_KEY']
    container_name =os.environ['STORAGE_CONTAINER_NAME']
    days_until_expiry = 365*5
    id_number = 'id1'
    container_permissions = ContainerPermissions.READ
    
    generate_SAS()
-------------------------------------------------------------------------

### Have you found a mitigation/solution?
you need to create access policies in the Azure portal, and in the script, just using token generation part to pass in the policy id to associate with it. It's not a solution, it's a work around.

Note: for table service, please post the issue here instead: https://github.com/Azure/azure-cosmosdb-python.

issue_title: check-docstring-first can't handle unicode
issue_body:
We have some application where there are Unicode characters (German umlauts, e.g. "ü") in some strings. Strangely I could not reproduce the issue in my dev environment, only in my testing environment.

I tested in a Docker container `buildpack-deps:xenial`.

```
# on the host
docker run -it buildpack-deps:xenial /bin/bash
# in the container
apt update && apt install python3-pip
# here I'd install pre-commit with other things via pip3
pre-commit install
pre-commit autoupdate
pre-commit run --all-files

Check docstring is first.................................................Failed
hookid: check-docstring-first

Traceback (most recent call last):
  File "/root/.pre-commit/repomdnrbb1l/py_env-python3/bin/check-docstring-first", line 11, in <module>
    load_entry_point('pre-commit-hooks==0.9.1', 'console_scripts', 'check-docstring-first')()
  File "/root/.pre-commit/repomdnrbb1l/py_env-python3/lib/python3.5/site-packages/pre_commit_hooks/check_docstring_first.py", line 60, in main
    contents = io.open(filename).read()
  File "/root/.pre-commit/repomdnrbb1l/py_env-python3/lib/python3.5/encodings/ascii.py", line 26, in decode
```

A (not so) quick [search for unicode characters](https://stackoverflow.com/questions/3001177/how-do-i-grep-for-all-non-ascii-characters-in-unix) resulted in:
```plain
ag -r "[\x80-\xFF]" .

260:        return "Nicht Gen��gend"
309:            return (4, "Gen��gend")
```

I am wondering if this is because `python --version` shows the 2.7 Python (`2.7.12`).

issue_title: When using `types`, why is it necessary to specify a noop `files`?
issue_body:
I'd expect something like this to match some files:

```yaml
-   repo: https://github.com/prettier/prettier
    sha: 1.10.2
    hooks:
    -   id: prettier
        name: prettier (scss, markdown)
        types: [markdown, scss]
```

As evidenced, it doesn't: https://i.fluffy.cc/mMkBxvs5XF8Lbf8bxxXFRxWClBk8Cg58.html

This is because we apparently also need to add a `files: ''`

Is this behavior intentional?

From reading [the docs](https://pre-commit.com/#filtering-files-with-types), it's not obvious that this is the case - maybe we could clarify this? :)

Thanks!

issue_title: Multidict preserves order- document it somewhere
issue_body:
As it is implemented now, Multidict preserves order.
Because of the way it is used in aiohttp (parsing post items and HTTP headers), this seems a very desirable property, and not merely an implementation detail.

I think it should be documented both here and in aiohttp's docs that Multidict preserves order, so that one doesn't need to read the implementation to be sure.

issue_title: Comment box should grow larger for larger comments
issue_body: If you're leaving a long comment for someone, it would be nice if the comment box automatically got bigger, as it's pretty small to begin with. I don't want to make all the comment boxes bigger, because that would take up too much space -- so having it get bigger only when there's a need for it would be great.

issue_title: Iframe strange behavior, duplicates full notebook inside a cell
issue_body:
Hi! Thanks for taking a look at this, and apologies ahead of time if this is problem is caused by something I shouldn't be doing in the first place rather an issue with notebook. I was unable to find a similar issue, nor a work around. I am trying to display some HTML within a notebook. To compartmentalize the CSS, I am using an iframe. Because I want to construct the HTML in the notebook rather than load it from a file, I am using the `IPython.display.HTML` function rather than `IPython.display.IFrame`. Whenever I click an interactive component, the entire notebook appears to be copied within the output cell. A minimal example is ``` Python import IPython import html raw_html = '''

Some HTML In an iframe

[-]
''' # In my actual case, this JS runs when the [-] is clicked. It is not necessary to reproduce this issue though. # d3.select("#meta_control").on("click", function() { # d3.select("#meta").style("display", "none"); # }); iframe = f'''''' IPython.display.HTML(iframe) ``` The output for this will look like ![before_click](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/5808961/37236883-6d320f14-23c1-11e8-8096-2a61e02d1600.png) and after clicking the `[-]` link, it looks like ![after_click](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/5808961/37236885-6ff70b00-23c1-11e8-8ecf-0970eee850bd.png) This issue does not happen if I load the same html from a file using either this method: ``` Python iframe = '' IPython.core.display.display(IPython.core.display.HTML(iframe)) ``` or this method ``` Python IPython.core.display.display(IPython.display.IFrame('test.html', width=500, height=500)) ``` where `test.html` contains the `raw_html` string defined above. I would be interested in contributing a fix and working more on this if someone with more understanding of the project could help me figure out what is going on. Thank you! Here is a notebook with all this code: [http://nbviewer.jupyter.org/gist/sauln/b4e5847d503f5760ab8c8683a5119ba3](http://nbviewer.jupyter.org/gist/sauln/b4e5847d503f5760ab8c8683a5119ba3)

issue_title: Getting IP of client in and filename in Notebook
issue_body:
I am trying to create a helper function in a notebook I am making which will automatically download the current notebook after running some ipywidget based visualizations.

The part I'm stuck at is: The notebook needs to be downloaded to the user's desktop by clicking a IPywidget button.

Is there any method I can use in the kernel to:
 - Get the name of the notebook the kernel is currently running ?
 - Get the IP of the client so I can do a `scp` (if I have access to do an scp)

I know there are methods where there can be 2 notebooks using the same kernel or 1 console 1 notebook using the same kernel - but I am making the assumption that there is only 1 notebook connected to a kernel for now.

issue_title: custom.js load order not the same if js compiled
issue_body:
using require js:
```
custom.js first hit
custom.js:12 in custom.js listen for events:
///
notebook.js:2504 notebook_loaded.Notebook trigger
```

Minified js:
```
custom.js first hit
...
notebook.js:2504 notebook_loaded.Notebook trigger
...
custom.js:12 in custom.js //  listen for events
```

Which make some things (like changing keymap) impossible with minified JS. 

Have a specific call to `custom.js:load` if defined ?

issue_title: Non-Entries/Comments are not interpreted correctly
issue_body:
According to [btxdoc](http://mirrors.ctan.org/biblio/bibtex/base/btxdoc.pdf), §4, item 7, p. 13

> BibTeX allows in the database files any comment that's not within an entry. If you want to comment out an entry, simply remove the `@` character preceding the entry type.

However when you parse the following database

```bibtex
@ARTICLE{bla,
    author={Fartsy},
    title={Title},
}

year=nonsense,
```

`year` gets added to the entry like

```python
{u'}\n\nyear': u'nonsense,', 'ID': 'bla', ... }
```

issue_title: Multiple generations of agents
issue_body:
I would like to use mesa for a project I am working on, but I am not sure how best to approach it. It will likely lead to new contributions to the base code, or at least an extension.

The idea is to have adult agents and child agents, add network connections between agents, children learn from adults, adults "die", children mature into adults, and then a new generation of child agents is created, and the process iterates again. At first, I thought that this would work best as a new kind of Scheduler, because it manages which agents are activated when. However, it also seems to be a special case of a BatchRunner, but where each new iteration is dependent on the previous iteration's output.

As for data collection in my case, the most important metrics would involve the final states of agents after each iteration.

Before I make a first draft, does anyone have any suggestions for the best way to build this?

issue_title: Add support for threaded test execution
issue_body:
Threads will help speed up I/O intensive or calculation intensive test suites such as end to end tests that perform HTTP requests, invoke methods from with C extensions etc.
See https://github.com/sindresorhus/ava as a good example for the speed gain.

I discussed this with @RonnyPfannschmidt and it seems that it would require a lot of work and thread safety from pytest itself.
This can be provided as a third party provided that pytest-xdist will provide a public extension API.
pytest-cloud is an example of such extension but as far as I know there's no detailed documentation that explains how to do so.

issue_title: endless loop in rrule.py
issue_body:
There is a bug in rrule.py (dateutil 2.3). The `while True` loop on line 659 does not always terminate:
```python
elif freq == HOURLY:
    if filtered:
        # Jump to one iteration before next day
        hour += ((23-hour)//interval)*interval
    while True:
        hour += interval
        div, mod = divmod(hour, 24)
        if div:
            hour = mod
            day += div
            fixday = True
        if not byhour or hour in byhour:
            break
```
Here's an example:
If the initial value of `hour` is an odd number, say `23`, and the `byhour` tuple is, e.g., `(0, 6, 12, 18)`, then we have an endless loop if `interval` is even because `hour` will always be odd and, hence, `hour in byhour` is never `True`!

Because dateutil is used by matplotlib, this causes some plotting routines to freeze.

I've recently observed a crash in libz3.so when I run z3 under very high memory pressure. The crash is in [small_object_allocator.cpp in Line 111](https://github.com/Z3Prover/z3/blob/master/src/util/small_object_allocator.cpp#L111). issue_title: get_class on arrays of text
issue_body:
When an attibute is defined with `dtype='text'`, `get_class` always expects a `str`, even if `shape` is defined, e.g. `shape=(None,1)`. In that case it should take a list of strings.

```python
from pynwb.spec import NWBDatasetSpec, NWBNamespaceBuilder, NWBGroupSpec, \
    NWBAttributeSpec

name = 'test'
ns_path = name + '.namespace.yaml'
ext_source = name + '.extensions.yaml'

test_obj = NWBGroupSpec(
    neurodata_type_def='TestObj',
    doc='test object',
    datasets=[
        NWBDatasetSpec(
            name='filler_data', doc='data doc', dtype='int', shape=(None, 1),
            attributes=[
                NWBAttributeSpec(
                    name='text_data', dtype='text',
                    doc='text_data doc',
                    shape=(None, 1))
            ]
        )
    ],
    neurodata_type_inc='NWBDataInterface')

ns_builder = NWBNamespaceBuilder(name + ' extensions', name)
ns_builder.add_spec(ext_source, test_obj)
ns_builder.export(ns_path)



from pynwb import load_namespaces, get_class

# load custom classes
load_namespaces(ns_path)
TestObj = get_class('TestObj', name)
TestObj(source='source', name='name', filler_data=[1], text_data='hello')
```

runs without error, but if you replace the last line with:

issue_title: __hash__ conflict due to duplicated creation_counter value
issue_body:
Hi,
I use https://github.com/jjkester/django-auditlog package in my project and I found, that sometimes `MoneyField` fields are not tracked well (change between old and new state is detected, but only `currency` is saved).
## Investigation

I try to debug what is wrong and found something very internal.

There is function in `auditlog`, which get list of model fields (https://github.com/jjkester/django-auditlog/blob/master/src/auditlog/diff.py#L31). Basically it just call Django's `instance._meta.get_fields()`. Then it make `set` from this list, for example here: https://github.com/jjkester/django-auditlog/blob/master/src/auditlog/diff.py#L80

The problem is, that before `set` I have list like this:

```
[<django.db.models.fields.AutoField: id>, 
<django.db.models.fields.related.ForeignKey: employee>, 
<django.db.models.fields.DateField: date>, 
<djmoney.models.fields.CurrencyField: amount_currency>, 
<django_enumfield.db.fields.EnumField: payment_type>, 
<djmoney.models.fields.MoneyField: amount>]
```

When auditlog put this list into `set`, I get just:

```
{<django_enumfield.db.fields.EnumField: payment_type>, 
<djmoney.models.fields.CurrencyField: amount_currency>, 
<django.db.models.fields.related.ForeignKey: employee>, 
<django.db.models.fields.AutoField: id>, 
<django.db.models.fields.DateField: date>}
```

As you could see, field `<djmoney.models.fields.MoneyField: amount>` is missing. The problem here is, that `<djmoney.models.fields.MoneyField: amount>` **return same hash number** as some other field in my model:

```
(Pdb) out[4]
<django_enumfield.db.fields.EnumField: payment_type>
(Pdb) out[4].__hash__()
264
(Pdb) out[5]
<djmoney.models.fields.MoneyField: amount>
(Pdb) out[5].__hash__()
264
```

(`out` is my temporary list of fields)

To be sure, I print also hashes of other fields:

```
(Pdb) [(i, i.__hash__()) for i in out]
[(<django.db.models.fields.AutoField: id>, -34), 
(<django.db.models.fields.related.ForeignKey: employee>, 261), 
(<django.db.models.fields.DateField: date>, 262), 
(<djmoney.models.fields.CurrencyField: amount_currency>, 263), 
(<django_enumfield.db.fields.EnumField: payment_type>, 264), 
(<djmoney.models.fields.MoneyField: amount>, 264)]
```

So I go deeper in Django internals.

According to https://code.djangoproject.com/wiki/DevModelCreation#InstallingTheAttributes, fields `__init__` call precede `contribute_to_class` (which in case of moneyfield call `add_currency_field` method). So, first all models fields are initialized (and unique `creation_counter` is evaluated) and only then `add_currency_field` is called.

With this knowledge, I think that current implementation of https://github.com/django-money/django-money/blob/master/djmoney/models/fields.py#L363-L375 is wrong. Particulary this two lines:

```
currency_field.creation_counter = self.creation_counter
self.creation_counter += 1
```

You assign `creation_counter` from `MoneyField` class to `CurrencyField` and then increase `self.creation_counter` **to value that is already attached to another field**. This way, two fields have same `creation_counter` which leads to same `__hash__` used in dicts, sets, etc (see https://github.com/django/django/blob/3c447b108ac70757001171f7a4791f493880bf5b/django/db/models/fields/__init__.py#L473-L474)
## Solution?

```
diff --git a/djmoney/models/fields.py b/djmoney/models/fields.py
index bd315dc..9458fc8 100644
--- a/djmoney/models/fields.py
+++ b/djmoney/models/fields.py
@@ -10,6 +10,7 @@ from django.core.exceptions import ImproperlyConfigured, ValidationError
 from django.db import models
 from django.db.models import F
 from django.db.models.signals import class_prepared
+from django.db.models.fields import Field
 from django.utils import translation

 from djmoney import forms
@@ -309,6 +310,9 @@ class MoneyField(models.DecimalField):

         super(MoneyField, self).__init__(verbose_name, name, max_digits, decimal_places, default=default, **kwargs)

+        self.creation_counter += 1
+        Field.creation_counter += 1
+
     def setup_default(self, default, default_currency, nullable):
         if default is None and not nullable:
             # Backwards compatible fix for non-nullable fields
@@ -369,8 +373,7 @@ class MoneyField(models.DecimalField):
             default=self.default_currency, editable=False,
             choices=self.currency_choices
         )
-        currency_field.creation_counter = self.creation_counter
-        self.creation_counter += 1
+        currency_field.creation_counter = self.creation_counter - 1
         currency_field_name = get_currency_field_name(name)
         cls.add_to_class(currency_field_name, currency_field)
```

I simply move forward `creation_counter` by 2 instead of 1 (first move in `Field.__init__`, second here) and **update global counter** (so next field in model have no conflict with our `MoneyField`. Meaning of this is that I save two values for our field (one for amount, second for currency).

Second modification is in `add_currency_field` method. Instead of accessing next `creation_counter` I use current and previous value (already saved in constructor).

But I am not sure about side effect. For example original `Field` operate also with `auto_creation_order` (https://github.com/django/django/blob/3c447b108ac70757001171f7a4791f493880bf5b/django/db/models/fields/__init__.py#L171-L176) but I really don't know what does it mean.

Please take a look.

Thanks!

issue_title: The correct way to use Reaching Definitions
Bug in claripy for negative FPs
issue_body:
So I think claripy (and consequently angr) has some issues with negative floating point numbers. It seems to acknowledge them internally, but at some points it can drop the sign.

Below I have an example of this happening, but in some of my code it happens deep in an AST and causes the AST to be Unsatisfiable, when really it shouldn't be.

```python
import angr
import claripy
from IPython import embed

def test1():
    print "Test 1:"
    x = claripy.FPS('x', claripy.FSORT_FLOAT)
    y = claripy.FPV(-1.0, claripy.FSORT_FLOAT)
    s = claripy.Solver()
    s.add(x == y)
    print s.satisfiable() #Prints out True
    print s.max(x) #Prints out 1.0 (Positive)
    print s.min(x) #Prints out 1.0 (Positive)

def test2():
    print "Test 2:"
    x = claripy.FPS('x', claripy.FSORT_FLOAT)
    w = claripy.FPV(1.0, claripy.FSORT_FLOAT)
    z = claripy.FPV(0.0, claripy.FSORT_FLOAT)
    s = claripy.Solver()
    s.add(claripy.fpEQ(claripy.fpAdd(claripy.fp.RM_RNE, x, w), z))
    print s.satisfiable() #Prints out True
    print s.max(x) #Prints out 1.0 (Positive)
    print s.min(x) #Prints out 1.0 (Positive)

def test3():
    print "Test 3:"
    b = angr.Project("/bin/true", load_options=dict(auto_load_libs=False))
    state = b.factory.entry_state()
    x = claripy.FPS('x', claripy.FSORT_FLOAT)
    y = claripy.FPV(1.0, claripy.FSORT_FLOAT)
    z = claripy.FPV(0.0, claripy.FSORT_FLOAT)
    state.add_constraints(claripy.fpAdd(claripy.fp.RM_RNE, x, y) == z)
    print state.se.satisfiable() #Prints out True
    print state.se.any_int(x) #Prints out 1.0 (Positive)


def main():
    test1()
    test2()
    test3()

if __name__=="__main__":
        main()
```

issue_title: Different encoding reading / writing headers?
issue_body:
Reading a WARC record (using `ArchiveIterator`) with a unicode character outside the range of `iso-8859-1` is fine, but writing it again (using WARCWriter) gives the error
```
UnicodeEncodeError: 'latin-1' codec can't encode character '\u2019' in position 336: ordinal not in range(256).
```

This is the header line causing the problem in this case:
```
Content-disposition: attachment; filename="Lancement du Système d’Échange Local (SEL).pdf"
```
with the `’` in `d’Échange` causing the problem.

[This code](https://github.com/webrecorder/warcio/blob/95d5dcd3cc3b61f6e276fcaf32ad1830e4211a72/warcio/statusandheaders.py#L277) probably parses the headers using `utf-8`:
```python
    def decode_header(line):
        try:
            # attempt to decode as utf-8 first
            return to_native_str(line, 'utf-8')
        except:
            # if fails, default to ISO-8859-1
            return to_native_str(line, 'iso-8859-1')

```
[These](https://github.com/webrecorder/warcio/blob/95d5dcd3cc3b61f6e276fcaf32ad1830e4211a72/warcio/warcwriter.py#L225) are the lines of code that write headers hardcoded in latin-1:
```python
def _set_header_buff(self, record):
    headers_buff = record.http_headers.to_bytes(self.header_filter, 'iso-8859-1')
    record.http_headers.headers_buff = headers_buff
```
If headers are by default read in `utf-8`, wouldn't it make sense to write them as `utf-8` as well?

issue_title: App crashes when using Sensor.Start(FrameCallback)
issue_body:
Hi
I'm using D415 on PC with .NET wrapper.
I prefer push model for consuming frames. The only API I've found for that is **Sensor.Start** method taking a callback delegate. However, after calling it the app crashes suddenly (after processing a few frames) and debug output contains message about invoking a garbage-collected delegate.

Digging into the code revealed that **Sensor.Start** actually creates another ad-hoc delegate which it then passes to unmanaged code. But because this ad-hoc delegate is not referenced by any managed object it becomes a subject for garbage collection after leaving the method body. So, after it is collected, calls from unmanaged code lead to error.

To mitigate this issue, a reference to delegate should be stored somewhere. Some options include:

- A private field in **Sensor** class. Storing a single delegate instance should be sufficient because no more than a single callback can be activated simultaneously. Care should be taken here regarding thread-safety.

- Make the **Start** method return some token (a "black-box" data structure) which holds a reference to the delegate. And require to pass this token in a subsequent invocation of **Stop** method. This should force a responsible user of the class to hold the token (effectively keeping the delegate alive) for a period when the callback can be actually called.

For the time being I've worked the issue around by making a replacement method for **Sensor.Start** in my code which calls unmanaged **rs2_start** and does proper work of storing a reference to a delegate passed.

issue_title: Unpinned dependency on graphql-core leads to issues
issue_body:
We use `graphene` 1.4.1 and haven't yet updated to the recently released 2.0. Because the setup.py [only specifies a minimum version for `graphql-core`](https://github.com/graphql-python/graphene/blob/v1.4.1/setup.py#L85), a recent deploy pulled in `graphql-core` 2.0 with `graphene` 1.4.1, versions which are incompatible and resulted in breaking changes.

This is related to a similar issue raised in `graphene-django` (https://github.com/graphql-python/graphene-django/issues/277).

Would you be willing to cut a 1.4.2 release that pinned `graphql-core` to `<2`?

issue_title: UDP port re-use for CoAP application client and server
issue_body:
Real-life issue: application, which hosts CoAP application server, sends a registration (or update) to CoAP resource directory (https://tools.ietf.org/html/draft-ietf-core-resource-directory-10) enabled service with success, however once the service tries to contact the application server, it uses the information from registration (update) including the client UDP port, which in this case is ephemeral and not the same as the application servers port.

Solution required: ability to send requests from CoAP server or at least the same (re-used) UDP source port.

Currently for testing purposes I've forced client port to be the same as application server's and in client's ProcessReceivedMessage I've moved the destructive `aMessage.MoveOffset(responseHeader.GetLength());` to be executed only when the message is really processed so that the server's ProcessReceivedMessage could handle the remaining messages. This does work, but is more like a workaround.
Another solution would be to merge client and server, but this requires lots of changes and as such would most likely introduce a bunch of other issues.

What do you think would be the best way in this situation?

issue_title: global variables not accessible when running scripts from embedded interpreter
k8s 1.5 support
issue_body:
Small change happend in the k8s 1.5 release, now it returns None in case there is nothing to return and in 1.4 it was a empty array.

I did a fast ugly patch which fix this for me:

```
diff -ur pykube/pykube/query.py /home/vagrant/.local/lib/python2.7/site-packages/pykube/query.py
--- pykube/pykube/query.py      2016-12-13 12:03:11.924150049 +0000
+++ /home/vagrant/.local/lib/python2.7/site-packages/pykube/query.py    2016-12-13 12:03:45.035794481 +0000
@@ -105,17 +105,21 @@
         Execute the API request and return an iterator over the objects. This
         method does not use the query cache.
         """
-        for obj in self.execute().json()["items"]:
-            yield self.api_obj_class(self.api, obj)
+        if self.execute().json()["items"] is not None:
+            for obj in self.execute().json()["items"]:
+                yield self.api_obj_class(self.api, obj)

     @property
     def query_cache(self):
         if not hasattr(self, "_query_cache"):
             cache = {"objects": []}
             cache["response"] = self.execute().json()
-            for obj in cache["response"]["items"]:
-                cache["objects"].append(self.api_obj_class(self.api, obj))
-            self._query_cache = cache
+            if cache["response"]["items"] is not None:
+                for obj in cache["response"]["items"]:
+                    cache["objects"].append(self.api_obj_class(self.api, obj))
+                self._query_cache = cache
+            else:
+                self._query_cache = {"objects": {}}
         return self._query_cache

     def __len__(self):
```

Pls note - it's 0.13.0 diff.

issue_title: ish keith
Certbot fails when using an invalid `/etc/hosts` configuration and does not print a specific diagnostic error.
issue_body:
I set up a server with an incorrect ip address associated with the host name of the server in its `/etc/hosts` file, causing `certbot-auto` to fail when attempting to create a certificate with the `--apache` method. It would be nice if certbot detected this specific issue and printed a helpful message specific to the error.

```
2016-12-07 16:55:49,372:DEBUG:certbot.reporter:Reporting to user: The following errors were reported by the server:

Domain: my.domain
Type:   connection
Detail: Failed to connect to 111.111.111.111:443 for TLS-SNI-01 challenge

To fix these errors, please make sure that your domain name was entered correctly and the DNS A record(s) for that domain contain(s) the right IP address. Additionally, please check that your computer has a publicly routable IP address and that no firewalls are preventing the server from communicating with the client. If you're using the webroot plugin, you should also verify that you are serving files from the webroot path you provided.
```

I set up my server using xen and a custom fai system, but using an incorrect ip address. I corrected the host's dns entry and fixed /etc/network/interfaces and the /etc/xen/my.domain.cfg file, but I did not change `/etc/hosts` until I debugged the source of the issue. To test this issue, you could try setting up Trisquel 7 or any GNU/Linux system and adding (or changing) an entry in /etc/hosts, inserting the incorrect ip address for the FQDN and PQDN of the host. As far as I know, the incorrect ip address I used was not in use by any other host on the internet at the time of testing.

As an aside, apache2 was serving http over the https port when incorrectly configured like this. You can see this by running:

    curl http://my.domain:443/

If you would like more details, see the following thread:

* https://community.letsencrypt.org/t/certbot-auto-unable-to-complete-tls-sni-01-challenge-on-ubuntu-14-04/23657

Thank you.

you can easily notice that the message he received is in the old format with `channel` instead of `channel_identifier.

## Task

The `channel` vs `channel_identifier` of this case may not be really important as not many peers would end up in this situation.

- [ ] Have a version field for all raiden messages
- [ ] Process all messages depending on the version
- [ ] Gracefully handle all cases where old messages or malformed message are received.

issue_title: Requests requirement.
issue_body:
Requests is a popular requirement. By fixing the version you require all other packages that want to depend on you to use the same version. Could the requirement be made more flexible?

And now as a counter argument to this request, pywebpush currently does not work with Requests 2.11.0 due to [the following change](https://github.com/kennethreitz/requests/blob/master/HISTORY.rst#2110-2016-08-08):

> Some previous releases accidentally accepted non-strings as acceptable header values. This release does not.

This breaks as `ttl` header defaults to the integer `0`.

issue_title: Simplifying rules for parameter
issue_body:
I am not happy with the behavior of parameter in [this example](http://vatlab.github.io/SOS/doc/documentation/Command_Line_Options.html#Redefinition-of-parameter-13)

```
[default_1]
parameter: cutoff=10
sh:
  echo ${cutoff}

[batch]
for cutoff in range(3):
    sos_run('default')
```

because of uncertain behavior of the `parameter` clause in

```
# cutoff = 10
sos run script default
# cutoff = 5
sos run script default --cutoff 5
# cutoff = 0, 1, 2, or 10?
sos run script batch
# cutoff = 0, 1, 2, or 5?
sos run script batch --cutoff 5
```

I am however not sure what we can do to simply the use of `parameter`.

issue_title: Change default faker locale in factory_boy
issue_body:
How can I set the default locale in Python's factory_boy for all of my Factories?

In docs says that one should set it with factory.Faker.override_default_locale but that does nothing to my fakers...

```
import factory
from app.models import Example
from custom_fakers import CustomFakers

# I use custom fakers, this indeed are added
factory.Faker.add_provider(CustomFakers)

issue_title: Incorrect parameters for Partition.mount_iso_image()
issue_labels: [{'id': 429586299, 'url': 'https://api.github.com/repos/zhmcclient/python-zhmcclient/labels/area:%20code', 'name': 'area: code', 'color': '207de5', 'default': False}, {'id': 429588767, 'url': 'https://api.github.com/repos/zhmcclient/python-zhmcclient/labels/resolution:%20fixed', 'name': 'resolution: fixed', 'color': 'cccccc', 'default': False}, {'id': 429587708, 'url': 'https://api.github.com/repos/zhmcclient/python-zhmcclient/labels/type:%20bug', 'name': 'type: bug', 'color': '84b6eb', 'default': False}]
issue_body:
There is a number of issues in the interface and implementation of the `Partition.mount_iso_image()` method:
- The method is currently defined to have input properties. However, the HMC operation 'Mount ISO Image' has no input properties that would be put into the request body, but instead the request body is the binary content of the image file.
- For this HMC operation, the `Content-Type` header must be set to `application/octet-stream` because the request body is binary data, but the current implementation does not set that header.
- This HMC operation has two query parameters (in the URL), which are not supported by the current interface and implementation of the method.

issue_title: Support project config values that don't have a delimiter in the key
issue_body:
We currently support specifying required config values in `Pulumi.yaml` under `template: config:`. Keys such as `aws:region` or `foo:bar` work great, but we don’t currently support keys that don't have a `:` delimiter, which prevents the use of `new pulumi.Config()` for application level config.

We should allow config keys without a delimiter and treat them the same as `pulumi config set` does, automatically prepending the project name to config keys that don't have a delimiter:

https://github.com/pulumi/pulumi/blob/f4e04e4a5d10c2e8b08a01fd0fab59e0ccdee37f/cmd/config.go#L306-L319

issue_title: Clean up after a driver exits.
issue_body:
Currently, if a driver submits a ton of long running tasks and then exits, those tasks will continue to be scheduled and will continue to execute. To see this, do the following.

1. Start a Ray cluster on one node.

    ```
    ./scripts/start_ray.sh --head --node-ip-address=127.0.0.1 --redis-port=6379
    ```

2. Connect a driver and submit a bunch of expensive tasks.

```python
import ray
import numpy as np

ray.init(redis_address="127.0.0.1:6379")

@ray.remote
def f():
  x = np.zeros((1000, 1000))
  for _ in range(10):
    np.dot(x, x)

[f.remote() for _ in range(1000)]
```

3. Then after the tasks have been submitted, kill the driver (e.g., with control-D to exit from Python).

4. Open up `top` or `htop` and see that the workers are busy running tasks.

When a driver exits, we should do the following:

- Don't execute any new tasks belonging to that driver.
- Kill any workers that are still executing tasks for that driver.
- Kill any actors that belong to that driver. This also requires releasing the resources associated with that actor.

We could do additional cleanup of state in Redis and in the workers (e.g., remote function definitions).

issue_title: trace: clashing RPCs share the same traceID
issue_body: I built code for a demo for "OpenCensus for Python gRPC developers" at https://github.com/orijtech/opencensus-for-grpc-python-developers and blogpost https://medium.com/@orijtech/opencensus-for-python-grpc-developers-9e460e054395 Given this code below ### Server ```python import grpc import os import time from concurrent import futures import defs_pb2_grpc as proto import defs_pb2 as pb # Tracing related imports from opencensus.trace.exporters import stackdriver_exporter from opencensus.trace.exporters.transports.background_thread import BackgroundThreadTransport from opencensus.trace.samplers import always_on from opencensus.trace.tracer import Tracer from opencensus.trace.ext.grpc import server_interceptor # Create the exporters: # 1. Stackdriver stackdriverExporter = stackdriver_exporter.StackdriverExporter( project_id=os.environ.get('OCGRPC_PROJECTID', 'census-demos'), transport=BackgroundThreadTransport) class CapitalizeServer(proto.FetchServicer): def __init__(self, *args, **kwargs): super(CapitalizeServer, self).__init__() def Capitalize(self, request, context): tracer = Tracer(sampler=always_on.AlwaysOnSampler(), exporter=stackdriverExporter) with tracer.span(name='Capitalize') as span: data = request.data span.add_annotation('Data in', len=len(data)) return pb.Payload(data=data.upper()) def main(): # Setup and start the gRPC server with the OpenCensus integration/interceptor tracer_interceptor = server_interceptor.OpenCensusServerInterceptor( always_on.AlwaysOnSampler(), stackdriverExporter) server = grpc.server( futures.ThreadPoolExecutor(max_workers=10), interceptors=(tracer_interceptor,)) proto.add_FetchServicer_to_server(CapitalizeServer(), server) server.add_insecure_port('[::]:9778') server.start() try: while True: time.sleep(60 * 60) except KeyboardInterrupt: server.stop(0) if __name__ == '__main__': main() ``` ## Client ```shell import grpc import defs_pb2_grpc as proto import defs_pb2 as pb def main(): channel = grpc.insecure_channel('localhost:9778') stub = proto.FetchStub(channel) while True: lineIn = input('> ') capitalized = stub.Capitalize(pb.Payload(data=bytes(lineIn, encoding='utf-8'))) print('< %s\n'%(capitalized.data.decode('utf-8'))) if __name__ == '__main__': main() ``` However, while running multiple clients to simulate traffic and popularity of a service, I noticed that a bunch of traces were bunched up together as viewed in the Stackdriver UI Bouncing around ideas with @sebright and @songy23, @sebright gave a postulation of perhaps the same traceID being shared which alas seems to the be problem, as per our log examinations This has a problem of bunching up totally different traces from different clients that are concurrently occuring and seems perhaps like a bug?

* active executions reference the hash of their process
* disable the version system

issue_title: support python2.7
Limits on twint.run.Profile
issue_body:
# Issue Template
Please use this template!

### Initial Check 
> If the issue is a request please specify that it is a request in the title (Example: [REQUEST] more features). If this is a question regarding 'twint' please specify that it's a question in the title (Example: [QUESTION] What is x?). Please **only** submit issues related to 'twint'. Thanks.

>Make sure you've checked the following:

- [x] Python version is 3.5 or higher.
- [x] Using the latest version of Twint.
- [x] I have searched the issues and there are no duplicates of this issue/question/request.

### Command Ran

import twint
c = twint.Config()
c.Username = "ferretscot"
c.Store_csv = True
c.Output = "ferretscot.csv"
twint.run.Profile(c)

***then***

import twint
c = twint.Config()
c.Username = "ferretscot"
c.Store_csv = True
c.Output = "ferretscot2.csv"
c.Profile_full = True
twint.run.Profile(c)

### Description of Issue
>Trying to get a comprehensive list of tweets and retweets for a given user. Twitters says example user given above has 7,292 tweets.

First command returns only 798 tweets and retweets (consistently). Earliest tweet returned is from 2017-10-07 (accounts first tweet was July 2013)

Second command is obviously much slower (30 minutes or so) but gets further back in time. However it also stops, this time at 2736 tweets, earliest tweet returned is from 2016-04-23.

Attempting to use Since and Until to collect earlier tweets than, for example, 2016-04-23, doesn't seem to work: it just begins returning data from present day.

### Environment Details
>MacOS High Sierra 10.13.5. Terminal. Python 3.6.5. pip twint module.
